title: Thyme steam inhalation
subtitle: Antimicrobial steam for nasal congestion
excerpt: Dried thyme steeped in a bowl of just-boiled water, inhaled as steam under a towel. Traditionally used for nasal congestion and upper respiratory infections.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, thyme entry: the expectorant and antiseptic tradition, the steam inhalation. the herbal reference on Thymi herba, the traditional use for coughs and upper respiratory infections.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dried thyme stores 12 months in a sealed tin, cool and dark. Prepare each session fresh.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that helps loosen and move mucus from the airways, making a cough more productive. Thyme's expectorant action is partly through direct relaxation of bronchial muscle and partly through the volatile oil's antimicrobial effect.

content.text: Steam inhalation with thyme is a simple home respiratory remedy. Boiling water draws the aromatic oil (thymol, carvacrol) out of the dried herb. Inhaling that steam delivers the

and antimicrobial action to exactly where it is needed. A large handful of dried thyme (about 10 g) steeped in a bowl of just-boiled water, then inhaled under a towel for 5-10 minutes, is the preparation. It can be repeated up to three times a day during an acute cold.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Steam burns. Use just-boiled water (taken off the heat first), not water still on the boil. Keep the towel-tent loose enough that you can pull it away immediately if the steam becomes too hot. Do not use with children under seven: the risk of steam burns is too high.

Pregnancy. Culinary thyme in cooking is standard and safe throughout pregnancy. A steam inhalation session with a large amount of thyme is outside the culinary dose range; consult your midwife before therapeutic steam inhalations in pregnancy.

Asthma. Steam itself can trigger bronchospasm in some asthma sufferers. If you have asthma, approach cautiously with a short first session and stop if breathing becomes more difficult.

items.prepNote: dried thyme; a large handful; fresh thyme works equally well
just off the boil; pour from the kettle then wait 1 minute

content.text: Place the thyme in a large bowl. Boil the kettle and let it rest 1 minute before pouring to bring the temperature down slightly from 100°C to about 90-95°C. Pour the hot water over the thyme. Sit at a table, drape a towel over your head and the bowl, and close your eyes. Inhale slowly through the nose, taking long, calm breaths, for 5-10 minutes. The steam should smell strongly of thyme from the first moment.

If the steam feels too intense, lift the towel edge to let some cool air in. The steam decreases naturally as the water cools. After the session, rest quietly for 10-15 minutes and do not go outside into cold air immediately.

One 5-10 minute session, up to three times a day during an acute upper respiratory infection. Use for the duration of the acute episode (typically 5-7 days). If a cough produces yellow or green mucus with fever for more than 3 days, see your doctor; this can indicate bacterial infection that may need antibiotics. Paediatric use: not for children under seven (steam burn risk); children aged 7-12 with adult supervision only, shorter session.
When not to use this remedy

Children under seven. Active asthma with poor control. Severe breathlessness, croup in a child, stridor (harsh high-pitched breathing sound), or any respiratory symptom that is rapidly worsening all need immediate medical attention.
